description:
You are a data-engineer who has just copied yesterday’s ETL results to the working directory
    /home/user/etl_data.  
    The directory already exists and contains three *immediate* sub-directories:  
      • /home/user/etl_data/raw  
      • /home/user/etl_data/processed  
      • /home/user/etl_data/temp  

    Each of those sub-directories already contains a handful of files (CSV, Parquet, TMP, etc.).
    Management wants a quick, reproducible snapshot of how much space (in bytes) each of those
    three sub-directories consumes so they can estimate future storage needs.

    Your task:
      1. Determine, for every *immediate* sub-directory of /home/user/etl_data, the total
         number of **bytes** occupied by all regular files that are directly inside that
         sub-directory (ignore any nested folders – there are none today, but the rule must
         hold).  
      2. Create the plain-text file  
            /home/user/etl_data_disk_usage.log  
         containing exactly three lines – one line per sub-directory – in strict
         alphabetical order of the sub-directory names.

         Output format (very strict, one space after the colon, no trailing spaces):
             raw: <INTEGER_BYTE_COUNT>
             processed: <INTEGER_BYTE_COUNT>
             temp: <INTEGER_BYTE_COUNT>

         For example, if the “raw” folder held two 500-byte files the first line would read  
             raw: 1000

         Requirements:
           • Units must be **bytes** (a raw integer, no commas, no “B”, no “bytes”).  
           • Use a single UNIX newline (LF) after every line (the last line may end
             with or without a final newline – both are acceptable).  
           • Do not include any extra blank lines, headers, or comments in the file.  

    Verification notes (what the automated grader will look for):
      • The file /home/user/etl_data_disk_usage.log exists.  
      • The file is world-readable (at least 0644).  
      • It contains exactly three lines whose names (“raw”, “processed”, “temp”) appear
        in alphabetical order and whose integer byte counts match the real sizes of the
        files currently present inside each corresponding sub-directory.

    Hints (you are free to choose any valid commands and pipelines):
      • Standard GNU/Linux utilities such as find, du, wc, and stat are available.  
      • Two or three concise commands are enough (calculate sizes → write the log file).
truth:
Initial filesystem state (before the agent starts):

    Directories:
      /home/user/etl_data/
      /home/user/etl_data/raw/
      /home/user/etl_data/processed/
      /home/user/etl_data/temp/

    Files and their exact byte sizes:

      /home/user/etl_data/raw/raw_1.csv            1024
      /home/user/etl_data/raw/raw_2.csv            2048

      /home/user/etl_data/processed/processed_2023-01.parquet   4096

      /home/user/etl_data/temp/temp_abc.tmp        512

    Therefore the correct byte totals that the grader will check are:
      raw:       3072
      processed: 4096
      temp:      512

    The grader will read /home/user/etl_data_disk_usage.log and compare its
    contents byte-for-byte (ignoring the optional trailing newline).
